coredata.dc:title: Surface oxygen engineered ZnCo2O4 planar hybrid supercapacitor electrode for high energy applications

coredata.dc:description: To enhance the electrochemical performances of supercapacitors, here suggested an unique strategy by creating oxygen vacancies on the electrode materials, which will progressively support for the pseudo as well as electrochemical double layer capacitance (EDLC). The laser treatment on the spinel ZnCo2O4 nanoleaves modified with functionalized MWCNT electrode have created surface O2 vacancies thereby midgap electronic states are generated. Illustrated, binding energy of (Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT) O1, O2 and O3 is increased, significantly about 0.8 eV higher for O2, similarly, the d-spacing is also increased to 0.24 nm which influences on electrochemical characteristics exhibiting 22 mA higher ipa (CV). Specific capacitance of the synthesized electrode materials H-60, H-80, H-100, ZCO@f-MWCNT, Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-3, Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6 and Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-9 are 683, 1129, 832, 1331, 1478, 2116 and 1780 F g−1 respectively was observed at 1A g−1 cd. The O2 vacancies created in 6 min (carbon nanodots decorated ZCO microspheres) exhibits a highest capacitance of 2116 F g−1 and retained 89–100 % coulumbic efficiency at various charge density 1A g−1-50 A g−1. The excellent electrochemical performance of Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6 electrode is due to the higher surface area and pore volume paves the way for improved ionic and redox capacitance. Furthermore, flexible solid-state hybrid planar supercapacitor was assembled with Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6 microspheres//active carbon (AC) exhibits a high energy density of 101.3 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 400.9 W kg−1 with 85 % retention of capacitance after 10,000 cycles. There was no legible variations in the CV pattern observed at 0, 30, 60, and 90° angles which shows the high stability of the device, interpreted no mechanical disintegration and the solid-state electrolyte illuminated LED for 7 min. Suggested that the oxygen vacancy creation is an efficient way to improve the electrodes performance in energy storage device application.

High-resolution XPS spectra of Zn 2p a (i, ii), Co 2p b (i, ii), O 1s c (i, ii) and C 1s d (i, ii) for ZCO@f-MWCNT and Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6 samples.

HR-TEM images of ZCO H-80 (a-c) and Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6 (d-f) in low and high resolution; SAED pattern in insert (c,f); Elemental distribution image and individual elements mapping of Zn, Co, O and C elements [g (i-v)].
Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms (a), pore size (k) distribution (b) of the ZCO H-80 and Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6 samples respectively.

Comparative CV (a) and GCD (b) graph of ZCO (H-60, 80 &100) and ZCO@f-MWCNT; Comparative CV (c), GCD (d), Specific capacitance bar diagram (e) EIS Spectra with fitted equivalent circuit (insert) (f) of Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT (3, 6 & 9 min) samples, CV at different scan rates and GCD curves at different current densities (g, h) of Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6 and The bar diagram for contribution of capacitance by diffusion and capacitive nature at various scan rates (i).

Schematic diagram of Hybrid Flexible Supercapacitor (a), CV curves of Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6 (positive) and AC (negative) electrodes in three electrode system at 10 mV s−1 (b), CV curves of AFSC at different scan rates (c), GCD curves of HFSC at different current densities (d), Specific capacitance (e), Power density vs. Energy density curve (f), Cycle stability of HFCS device for 10,000 cycles at 20 A g−1 (g), EIS spectra of HFCS device at 1st and after 10,000th cycles with equivalent circuit (h).

Digital images of the constructed HFSC device at different angles (a); CV curves of the HFSC device during different bending angles at 10 mV s−1 scan rate (b); GCD curves of series connected devices at a current density of 20 A g−1; Photograph of LED glowing for 5 min and 7 min (d, e) using fabricated HFSCs Device.
Rs and Rct values of ZCO H-80, ZCO@f-MWCNT & Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT (3, 6 & 9 min) samples are calculated from EIS Spectra.
Comparative performance of the HFSC Device with various binary Spinel metal oxide based Symmetric/Asymmetric devices.

head.ce:title: Surface oxygen engineered ZnCo2O4 planar hybrid supercapacitor electrode for high energy applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To enhance the electrochemical performances of supercapacitors, here suggested an unique strategy by creating oxygen vacancies on the electrode materials, which will progressively support for the pseudo as well as electrochemical double layer capacitance (EDLC). The laser treatment on the spinel ZnCo2O4 nanoleaves modified with functionalized MWCNT electrode have created surface O2 vacancies thereby midgap electronic states are generated. Illustrated, binding energy of (Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT) O1, O2 and O3 is increased, significantly about 0.8 eV higher for O2, similarly, the d-spacing is also increased to 0.24 nm which influences on electrochemical characteristics exhibiting 22 mA higher ipa (CV). Specific capacitance of the synthesized electrode materials H-60, H-80, H-100, ZCO@f-MWCNT, Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-3, Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6 and Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-9 are 683, 1129, 832, 1331, 1478, 2116 and 1780 F g−1 respectively was observed at 1A g−1 cd. The O2 vacancies created in 6 min (carbon nanodots decorated ZCO microspheres) exhibits a highest capacitance of 2116 F g−1 and retained 89–100 % coulumbic efficiency at various charge density 1A g−1-50 A g−1. The excellent electrochemical performance of Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6 electrode is due to the higher surface area and pore volume paves the way for improved ionic and redox capacitance. Furthermore, flexible solid-state hybrid planar supercapacitor was assembled with Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6 microspheres//active carbon (AC) exhibits a high energy density of 101.3 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 400.9 W kg−1 with 85 % retention of capacitance after 10,000 cycles. There was no legible variations in the CV pattern observed at 0, 30, 60, and 90° angles which shows the high stability of the device, interpreted no mechanical disintegration and the solid-state electrolyte illuminated LED for 7 min. Suggested that the oxygen vacancy creation is an efficient way to improve the electrodes performance in energy storage device application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The hunt for more efficient electrode materials in Electrical Vehicle (EV) technology is received enormous scientific attention in the whole world. Li-ion battery (LIB) is one among the currently used energy storage devices, however, LIBs have many limitations like low power density, unsafe, high cost and toxic [1]. Hence, most of the energy researchers are focusing on developing high energy density supercapacitors due to its inherent high power density, high rate capabilities, and extremely long cycle stability, which will be one of the finest prospects for the next generation energy storage devices [2]. On the other hand, cathode electrode material of LIBs plays an important role for high energy density supercapacitors [3–5].

Three-dimensional nanostructures are extremely attractive as electrodes due to their large specific surface area, which maximizes the number of ion-accessible active sites, and increased ion and electron transport, which facilitates reaction kinetics. The highly porous structure of the 3D transition metal oxide-based supercapacitor electrodes allows for increased active sites, accessible areas for electrolyte diffusion, and a greater area of interaction between the electrode and the electrolyte, which all contribute to the superlative electrochemical performance.

A complex metal oxides are generally described as AxB3-xO4, where A and B is a divalent & trivalent cation, respectively. The bimetallic spinel oxide electrodes have received significant attention for energy storage applications due to their low toxicity, versatility in morphological modification employing facile synthesis procedures, stable chemical composition, good electrochemical behavior, and long cycle life [6–8]. It's also significant to note, that the bimetallic cobaltite (MCo2O4, where M = Zn, Ni, Cu, Mn, Mg, & Fe) spinel oxide structures have received most attention because of not only lowers the electron transporting activation energy but also exhibit excellent physicochemical and electrochemical properties as compared to other monometallic oxides [9,10]. Among the various types of spinel electrodes, ZnCo2O4 (ZCO) has very high theoretical capacitance of 2604 F g−1 as well as low cost and nontoxic nature with a stable cyclability, thus ZCO has been considered as a superior electrode for supercapacitor applications [11–14].

To improve the electrochemical performances and flexibility of the ZCO's, addition of carbonaceous material is an easiest way to make flexible spinel oxide material, which will accommodate more charge carriers with in the composite materials [15–17]. The NiCo2O4/ZnCo2O4@graphene/carbon nanotubes (NZ@GC) electrode has showed a capacitance of 1128.6 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 with 84 % capacity retention after 6000 cycles [18] and a ZnCo2O4/ZnCo2O4/CoO@rGO/GO (ZCG-2) composite material was reported to be a specific capacitance of 1255 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. An asymmetric supercapacitor with ZCG-2//AC has exhibited a specific capacitance of 238.8 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, and a capacity retention rate of 85.5 % after 10,000 cycles. More importantly, at a power density of 800 W kg−1, and specific energy of 84.9 Wh kg−1 was reported [19].

A lattice imperfection and vacancy creation in the metal oxides are also another effective way to improve the electrochemical performance of the electrode materials [20–22]. There are numerous advantages on adding oxygen vacancies (Ovs) in the electrode materials such as significant level of electron active sites are created which is helpful for more ionic carries and mobility, in turn speedy Faradaic redox reaction consequent improved electrochemical performances are result [23,24]. Oxygen vacancies can effectively create an internal electric field at the atomic level, will significantly boost the electrochemical performances. However, the vacancies can alter the chemical and structural properties of the materials, hence, the active electrodes have to be constructed without compromising the structural stability [25–27]. In the present area, researchers deal with several kind of techniques for creating oxygen vacancies in metal oxides such as wet chemical reduction [28], plasma treatment [29], inert gas treatment [30], heteroatom doping [31] and laser irradiation methods. Among them, CO2 laser irradiation technique is promising one for creating Ovs on the surface of the electrode material.

Based on the above, the laser beam irradiated ZCO/porous rGO has shown higher oxygen defects on its surface [32]. A one-step laser treatment has been utilized for reduction and fragmentation to create O2 vacancy Co3O4 nanoparticles/graphene composites and exhibits a 93.7 % capacitance retention in the Supercapacitor [33]. From these view, understood that the oxygen vacancies created through the laser irradiation approach are more effective method for enhancing the energy storage performance and cycle life of the prepared electrodes in the supercapacitor applications.

Compared to sandwich type supercapacitors, the Micro supercapacitor (MCs) technology has recently become a more specialized field to exploit the electrochemical active electrodes [34,35]. MCs with in-plane interdigitated design have several advantages over conventional sandwich-structured supercapacitors, These planar structures can be easily integrated, which show high potential for future miniaturized energy storage devices and their short gap between electrically isolated interdigitated electrodes serves as their most notable benefits makes it simple to incorporate various electronic components into chips. The interdigitated electrode provides a large number of open edges, which can facilitate the diffusion of electrolyte ions. Mainly, by minimizing the inter-finger distance, it is possible to further reduce the ion diffusion resistance, which enhances energy density, power density and cycle life. Here, the prepared porous ZCO microspheres composite with functionalized MWCNT (f-MWCNT) using low temperature hydrothermal method is reported. The laser irradiation process on the surface of the prepared ZCO@f-MWCNT-6 electrode materials has been made to create the oxygen vacancy as optimized level in the composite (Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT). All the synthesized electrode materials were characterized for their structural, chemical, morphological and electrochemical properties exhibits excellent results. Finally, a high-performance Hybrid Flexible Supercapacitor (HFSC) was assembled by using the Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6//AC with solid-state gel electrolyte, which shows a high energy and power density and confirmed the device is suitable for portable applications [36–38].

The low temperature hydrothermal route was followed for the synthesis of spinel ZnCo2O4 microspheres using Zinc and Cobalt Nitrate sources. Typically, 2 mmol of Zn(NO3)2 6H2O and 4 mmol of Co(NO3)2. 6H2O were dissolved in 100 ml of DI water and then 60 mmol of urea (CH4N2O) was added to it under constant stirring. Then the mixture solution was transferred to teflon autoclave and kept at 80 °C in an oven for 12 h. After cooling the solution to room temperature, the slightly purple colored precipitate was collected by centrifugation and it was washed repeatedly with DI water and ethanol to remove impurity if any as well as the unreacted ions. The collected precipitate was further calcinated at 200 °C with 2 °C/min heating rate for 10 h to form the porous spinel ZCO microspheres [38]. The same procedure was used to synthesis porous ZCO microspheres at different hydrothermal temperature (60 °C and 100 °C). The ZCO microspheres synthesized at different low temperatures (60, 80 and 100 °C) are denoted as ZCO H-60, ZCO H-80 and ZCO H-100.

The, functionalization of MWCNT was done with acids mixture (H2SO4:HNO3) (Detailed procedure is given in supplementary information). The f-MWCNT: ZCO at 1: 9 ratio were taken in 100 ml DI water, stirred for 60 min for homogeneous mixing and then the hydrothermal procedure at 80 °C was followed as used for the synthesis of ZCO porous microspheres. Finally, the ZCO-80@f-MWCNT composite powder was centrifuged and washed with DI water and ethanol. After which, it was dried and calcinated at above said temperature and stored in the airtight sample vial for further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxygen vacancy created by laser engraved method
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared ZCO@f-MWCNT composite sample was coated onto a polyamide sheet and exposed to CO2 laser (Trotec, speedy 100 model instrument, laser power 2 W) at different time period of 3, 6, and 9 min. Finally, the oxygen-vacant generated electrode material was then taken off and utilized for further examinations.

The working electrode slurry was made by the combination of the synthesized electrode materials (80 %), acetylene black conducting materials (10 %) and PVDF binder (10 %) with few drops of 2-NMP solutions. The electrode slurry was subsequently coated on to the Ni- foam and dried at 80 °C for 12 h (mass of the working material is 3.2 mg) and its electrochemical performance was studied in 3 M Potassium hydroxide (KOH) with Ag/AgCl reference and platinum wire counter electrodes. Electrochemical workstation CHI 660D was utilized for investigating the electrochemical characterizations which include Cyclic Voltammetry (CV), Galvanostatic Charge/Discharge, and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) (frequency range of 10–100 kHz).

The ZCO synthesized by using hydrothermal method at 60, 80 and 100 °C have been analyzed for their crystal structure through XRD and the results are shown in Fig. S1 a. The lattice planes (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) were at 2θ values of 31°, 36°, 44°, 56°, 59°, and 65°, respectively, well matched with the JCPDS card No: 23–1390 [39,40]. Among the temperature used for the synthesis of ZCO, the ZCO H-80 sample exhibited strong diffraction peaks and a well-defined crystalline structure. Further to increase the conductivity and electrochemical performance of ZCO, the ZCO H-80 sample were combined with f-MWCNT and exposed to CO2 laser at various durations of 3, 6, and 9 min to create oxygen vacancy, which is expected to offer higher energy storage. The average crystallite size of the synthesized particles is 4.34, 8.14 and 12.23 nm for H-60, H-80 and H -100 samples respectively (Table S1). The f-MWCNT is fragmented to smaller shapeless particles in the nano size [41]. Hence, the peak for (002) plane is appeared at 2θ value of 25.8o. On the laser treatment of the ZCO@f-MWCNT, the f-MWCNT is changed its shape to sphere like carbon dots, observed from the high intensity peaks appeared at 21.9o 2θ angles shown in Fig. 1 [42]. Out of the three laser treatments (3, 6 and 9 min), the ZCO-MWCNT-6 sample demonstrated favorable conditions for achieving high crystalline phases where the crystallite size is approximately 0.14–0.20 nm lower than the other two materials (Table S1). However, some high crystalline phases were disrupted by the laser treatment beyond 6 min. The XRD pattern of Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6 represent the optimal possibility of achieving high crystalline phase formation since the ZCO phases are well-distinguished in this pattern (Fig. S1 c) [43]. Raman spectroscopy is another effective method for evaluating the unique structural properties of carbon-based materials. Fig. S1 c shows the Raman spectra of the f-MWCNT, ZCO and Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT composite samples, where two peaks at 522 cm−1 and 695 cm−1 band can be assigned to the F2g and A1g symmetrical modes ZCO microspheres [44]. The Raman spectra of the f-MWCNT sample exhibit two peaks, representing the D and G bands at 1340 and 1578 cm−1, respectively. The G bands arise as result of CC stretching bonds in the carbon structure, whereas the D band is caused by dispersion from localized carbon defects or disorders. The spectra for the Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT composite present the D and G bands of Carbon sources and F2g and A1g bands of ZCO microspheres, where no remarkable changes in the band position was noticed for the carbon, whereas a clear band shift for ZCO was observed (F2g-27 cm−1 and A1g- 12 cm−1), interpreting the composite is chemically bonded with f-MWCNT which will offer strength to the composites [45].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to determine the chemical compositions and oxidation states of ZCO@f-MWCNT and Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6 samples. The survey spectra (Fig. S1 d) confirmed the presence of Zn, Co, O, and C in both samples. Additionally, the Zn 2p spin-orbit lines at 1044.5 (2p1/2) and 1021.4 (2p3/2) eV with a separation of 23.1 eV confirmed Zn2+ state [Fig. 2a (i)]. Laser-assisted oxygen vacancies generation leads to the red (positive) shift spin orbit line, which disturb the Zn‑oxygen interactions, as observed in Fig. 2 a (ii) [46,47]. The Co 2p spectra exhibited peaks at 794.9 eV and 780.2 eV, along with two shake-up satellites at 802.6 eV and 785.4 eV, confirming the presence of Co2+ [Fig. 2 b (i)]. Following laser treatment, the Co 2p3/2 peak slightly positive shifted to 780.7 eV, indicating low coordination Co centers interacting with lattice planes [48]. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2 d) showed peaks at 284.4 eV (sp2 CC) and 284.9 eV (sp3 CO and O-C=O), and after laser treatment, the binding energy increased to 284.9 eV [49]. O 1s spectra [Fig. 2 c (ii)] revealed three peaks at 529.6 eV (O1), 530.8 eV (O2), and 533.6 eV (O3), indicating Metal (O=C) oxygen, oxygen vacancies (Z-O-C), and structural water molecules (O-C=O), respectively. Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT XPS spectra exhibited an increased O2 peak intensity and decreased O3 peak intensity, indicating oxygen vacancies present on the material's surface [50]. Laser treatment induced significant lattice rearrangement, resulting from a mixture of crystalline and amorphous nature. Oxygen content in relation to Zn and Co was decreased by the laser action, ultimately enhancing the material's intrinsic conductivity and specific capacitance. Binding energy values for ZCO@f-MWCNT & Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT (3, 6 & 9 min) are given in the Table S2.

The morphology of the synthesized ZCO H-80 & Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT materials were investigated using High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) technique and presented in Fig. 3 . The HR-TEM images (Fig. 3 (a,b)) revealed the porous natured of homogeneous microspheres, formed through the self-assembly of nano leaves. Fig. 3(d,e) shows that the laser-assisted oxygen vacancy creates microspheres connected with graphene-like leave structure, smeared carbon beads, which formed to high surface area and efficient electron and ion transport for improved electrochemical performance in supercapacitor applications [51,52]. Particularly, the addition of f-MWCNT did not significantly alter the micro size of ZCO H-80. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of ZCO H-80 is inserted in Fig. 3(c). The SAED patterns revealed a poly-crystalline nature with distinct diffraction rings, corresponding to the (311), (400), and (511) planes of the ZCO H-80 sample. The lattice spacing was calculated to be approximately 0.234 nm for the (311) plane, in before laser treatment (Fig. 3c). Laser irradiation induced slight alterations in the interplanar distance (0.24 nm), likely due to local deformation or stress, confirming the impact on oxygen content (Fig. 3f). This d-spacing expansion may enhance ionic mobility in the material. The ZCO H-80 sample exhibited a poly-crystalline nature, characterized by three concentric diffraction rings corresponding to the (311), (400), and (511) planes. Laser irradiation is induced a slight alterations in the interplanar distance, potentially enhancing ionic mobility [53]. SEM images of ZCO samples (H-60, H-80, and H-100) presented in Fig. S2 revealed uniform microspheres formed through the self-assembly of nano leaves, with calculated average particle sizes of 6.3 μm (ZCO-H60), 7.2 μm (ZCO-H80), and 8.9 μm (ZCO H-100) calculated by using Image J software. On comparing the SEM images of the ZCO (H-60, 80 &100) with ZCO@f-MWCNT, the microspheres are formed through the nanoleaves of ZCO and f-MWCNT smeared ZCO nanoleaves, the f-MWCNT is dismantled into nanoparticles shown in Fig. S2 (g,h). Laser treated ZCO@f-MWCNT-6, morphology is presented in Fig. 3 (d-f), indicated the carbon dots are formed during the laser exposure from the f-MWCNT which was confirmed by XRD analysis, whereas there is no remarkable changes observed in the ZCO nanoleaves, predicated that the synthesized ZCO@f-MWCNT-6 electrode is highly stable with uniform dispersion of carbon dots, confirmed through HR-TEM images. Laser treatment of MWCNTs leads to the unzipping of their tube-like structure, fragmented into multiple parts due to the breakage of SP2 bonds in the graphene, fragmented carbon atoms re-organise to form carbon dots. Moreover, Fig. 3 g (i–v) showed the Elemental distribution images obtained from TEM, which showed that Zn, Co, C, and O was uniformly distributed on the surface of the synthesized Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6 electrode material.

The specific surface area and pore size of electrode materials are play a crucial role in the performance of supercapacitors, BET study of the synthesized ZCO H-80 and Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT materials (Fig. 4 a, b) was made to find the pore size distribution and the surface area and pore properties. Both materials exhibited type IV hysteresis loops, indicating a mesoporous structure. However, the surface area of Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT was approximately 25 % higher than that of ZCO H-80 (Table S3), and the pore volume also increased due to the dispersion of f-MWCNT nanodots in the carbon media [54,55]. These modifications were significant but did not result in substantial physical changes in the composite material. The larger surface area and pore size of ZCO@f-MWCNT made it advantageous for maintaining structural stability during the intercalation/de-intercalation process, potentially leading to improved electrochemical performance by facilitating electrolyte penetration and close contact at the electrode/electrolyte interface.

The ZCO electrode materials are synthesized at different hydrothermal temperature (ZCO- H-60, H-80, and H-100) electrode materials systematically examined through Cyclic Voltammetry (CV), Galvanostatic charge-discharge studies, and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) in a three-electrode electrochemical cell system for their electrochemical performance with the potential range of 0.0 to 0.45 V in 3 M KOH electrolyte and CV studies was carried out at various scan rates 10–100 mV s−1 (Fig. S3).The CV curves of ZCO-60, 80 and 100 °C electrode materials exhibit a set of well defined redox peaks 360, 328, 324 mV anodic and 276, 220 and 236 mV cathodic peaks in it, 60,80 and 100 °C electrode materials with peak separation of 124, 103 and 88 mV respectively. On increasing the scan rate to 100 mV s−1 with the step values of 10 mV s−1, the anodic peak potential shifted towards positive value with not much difference in the ZCO H-60 anodic peak potential of lower scan rates upto 50 mV s−1 whereas other two electrodes show 7–12 mV variations. The ipa values are increased to 65.2, 84 & 73 mA for 60,80 and 100 °C electrode materials on increasing the scan rate to 100 mV s−1, with highest increment of the ipa is 66.2 mA for the H-80 electrode material was obtained. Similarly, the cathodic peak potential gradually shifted to lower potential values of 168, 193 and 197 mV with the potential difference of 32, 27 and 39 mV for the 60, 80 and 100 °C electrode materials respectively. The presence of a sharp redox peak substantiates rapid redox activity. Generally, across all temperatures and scan rates, the existence of redox peaks, particularly for ZCO H-60, seemed less favorable for the present applications. Among the electrode materials, ipc value is higher for the 80 °C electrode with lower Epa and Epc values with excellent pesudocapacitance values. The high current values indicating the enhanced ionic mobility within the matrix, even though all electrode materials exhibit good CV pattern, ZCO H-80 provided good pseudo capacitive behaviors. With increasing scan rates, the CV curve area is expanded, and the anodic peaks (0.32 V) shifted slightly towards more positive potentials (0.38 V), indicating facilitated reaction kinetics during charging. The marginal shift in redox peaks at different sweep rates can be attributed to a polarization effect, implying a decrease in ion availability [56]. Overall, the presence of reversible peaks remained consistent across all scan rates and cycles, indicative of capacitive behavior.

The GCD performance of the synthesized electrode materials at different current densities from 1 A g−1 to 5 A g−1 in the potential range of 0.0 to 0.45 V were made and their obtained data are presented in Fig. S3 (d-f). The specific capacitance values are compared, shown the Fig. S 4C bar diagram. The Specific capacitance at 1 A g−1 current density is 683, 1129 and 832 F g−1 for the electrode ZCO (H-60, 80 and 100) obtained respectively. The specific capacitances are decreased gradually (246, 722 and 435 F g−1) on increasing the charge/discharge current density to 5 A g−1 with an incremental value of 1 A g−1. A higher specific capacitance value of 1129 F g−1 is obtained for the ZCO H-80 electrode where as the other two electrode materials H-60 and H-100 showed a 446 F g−1 and 297 F g−1 less specific capacitance, which is due to the delayed redox – activity and strong ionic adsorption effect. The increase of charge/discharge rate, the effective ionic diffusion and double layer ionic moments are not much favorable for the 10 % utilization with the electrode material as it requires faster rate of diffusion. Hence, the specific capacitances are reduced to 36 % and 52 % for the H-60 and H-100 respectively. Whereas the H-80 electrode material retained its specific capacitance with 722 F g−1 (63 %) even at the highest current density of 5 A g−1, which indicate that the electrode material synthesized at 80 °C (ZCO H-80) hydrothermal routine could be an excellent choice for further development as observed in the CV results. The discharge plateau arises from faradic reactions are reversible in nature. In high-rate cycling, the capacity decreases due to the hindered deep ion diffusion, causing only the electrode's surface to be effectively utilized. The enhanced capacitance observed in sample ZCO H-80 possibly indicates ideal redox activity. Other samples may show delayed redox activity or strong adsorption effects and does not exhibit any predominant redox peak. At a current density of 1 A g−1, the bare ZCO H-80 electrode was found to possess a higher specific capacitance (1129 F g−1) compared to the other two electrodes (ZCO H-60 & H-100) with capacitances of 682 F g−1 and 832 F g−1 respectively, shown in Fig. S4 c.

The introduction of f-MWCNT into the composite electrode material (ZCO@f-MWCNT), resulting expanded potential window of 0.0–0.5 V scan rate 50 mV s−1 which is higher than the potential window of ZCO H-80 as evidenced in Fig. 5 a, it is due to the f-MWCNT to the microsphere particle surfaces in the bare ZCO H-80 matrix leads to the establishment of a stable potential window of 0.5 V [57]. This effect indicates that the inclusion of f-MWCNT enhances the redox reaction facilitation of ZCO, attributed to the improved conductivity introduced by f-MWCNT. This trend aligns with the general principle that the incorporation of carbonaceous materials can enhance the mechanical properties of oxide materials, imparting greater flexibility to the ionic mobility of oxides [58,59]. The cyclic voltammetry of ZCO@f-MWCNT was taken at 50 mV s−1 scan rate and presented in Fig. 5 a, indicate the ≈10 mA higher for the MWCNT electrode, than the H-80 electrode (55 mA). The CV curves of ZCO@f-MWCNT at various scan rate (10–100 mV s−1) (Fig. S4 a) shows gradual increase in current and potential, the increment is higher at lower scan rate and lower at higher scan rates noticed in the previous electrodes without the f-MWCNT addition. Charge transfer resistance of the ZCO H-80 and ZCO@f-MWCNT electrode materials was investigated through Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS), a reduced Rct of 2.15 Ω was obtained which is ≈ 50 % lower than the Rct of ZCO H-80 electrode, similarly 50 % reduced RS value also observed from Fig. S4 d, these result follows as in the CV results.

The charge/discharge performance characteristics of the ZCO@f-MWCNT was studied and their results are presented in Fig. S4 b (1–20 A g−1 current density). At 1 A g−1 current density, 1331 F g−1 specific capacitance was obtained, at the higher current density value of 20 A g−1, exhibited 658 F g−1 specific capacitance, which is about ≈50 % retention when compared to lower current density of 1 A g−1. The specific capacitance values of the electrodes taken at 1 A g−1 current density are compared (Fig. 5 b), can easily visualized, found 213 F g−1 higher capacitance was shown by the ZCO H-80 and f-MWCNT, where ZCO H-80 shown ≈1130 F g−1, bar chart for all the electrodes prefect manner 1–5 A g−1 given in Fig. S4 c.

A comparison of Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT (with treatment times of 3, 6 and 9 min) and untreated ZCO @f-MWCNT electrode materials reveals distinctive spindle-like shapes in their cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves. Notably, a marked shift in curve morphology occurs at specific peak positions, 0.36 V (anodic) and 0.2 V (cathodic), signifying discernible redox activity and concurrent variations in current density. The CV curve resulting from laser treatment demonstrates well-defined reduction and broad oxidation peaks. This trend underscores the heightened redox reactivity of cobalt and zinc within the KOH electrolyte, substantiated by the observed current intensity. The laser-treated electrode exhibits substantial charging and discharging capacity, likely attributed to augmented metal oxide interactions facilitated by laser irradiation, potentially influencing oxygen-related processes [60]. Although the potential window experiences a more, yet noteworthy, positive shift (cathodic peak), this alteration of this modified electrode could potentially provide an expanded potential window compared to the unmodified counterpart. Additionally, an investigation of cyclic voltammetry (CV) plots encompassing diverse scan rate (ranging from 10 mV s−1 to 100 mV s−1) was conducted to elucidate the electrochemical diffusion phenomena and capacitive-controlled processes intrinsic to the Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6 electrode material, as depicted in Fig. 5 (i). The rationale behind these observations regarding the diffusion and capacitive aspects of the Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6 electrode is expounded upon the accompanying supplementary materials. On oxygen vacancy creation, the charge transfer and ionic diffusion are enhanced only 0.65 Ω Rct value (Fig. 5 f). The RS & Rct values of all the prepared electrodes are given in the Table 1 , The Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6 electrode shown lower compared to other electrodes they (3 & 9 min irradiated) exhibited higher Rct values.

The enhanced electrical conductivity introduced by MWCNT into the host material contributes to a more efficient redox reaction within the specified voltage range. Electrochemical discharge-charge assessments were carried out on electrode materials subjected to laser treatment, depicted in Fig. 5 h. Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) profiles were acquired at varying current densities (1 A g−1 to 50 A g−1) for all the electrode materials, illustrated in Fig. S5 (c,d). Notably, at a current density of 1 A g−1, the Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6 electrode exhibited a higher specific capacitance of 2116 F g−1, surpassing the values recorded for the other two laser-treated electrodes with different time intervals: 1555 F g−1 (3 min) and 1780 F g−1 (9 min) (Fig. 5 e). The Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6 electrode demonstrates a significantly elevated capacitance compared to previously reported spinel-based electrodes (Fig. S6). Additionally, the laser-treated Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6 electrode was subjected to 10,000 electrochemical cycles, exhibiting a 95 % capacitance retention at 30 A g−1, illustrated in Fig. S5 (e). In Fig. S4 d, the unmodified ZCO H-80 electrode is characterized by a solution resistance (RS) of 0.86 Ω and a charge transfer resistance (Rct) of 4.19 Ω. Analysis of the polarized region, particularly within the mid-frequency range, reveals the presence of restricted ion mobility at the electrode surface. This constraint is evidenced by the observation of an elongated or suppressed semi-circle in the mid-frequency region. Furthermore, the diffusion process, which predominantly initiates around 1 kHz, demonstrates a non-narrow behavior, as indicated by a warburg angle exceeding 45°. Despite this, a suppressed semi-circle is still evident. In contrast, the impedance curve for the f-MWCNT-treated ZCO (depicted in Fig. S4(d)) showcases a distinct and well-defined semi-circle extending from high to mid frequencies, along with a narrow tail end characteristic of warburg diffusion. Corresponding values of RS and Rct amount to 0.49 Ω and 2.15 Ω, respectively, reflecting reduced resistances attributed to the conductive f-MWCNT incorporation. The introduction of carbon material enhances the electrode's capacitance, a phenomenon discernible at the tail end. Notably, the diffusion segment of ZCO@f-MWCNT exhibits behavior to an electrical double layer (EDL) due to its constrained width, evident by the warburg angle approximating 90° a contrast to the behavior of the bare ZCO. The observed amelioration of polarization effects within the mid-frequency range implies an enhancement in ion mobility at the electrode surface due to the inclusion of carbon material. This behavior aligns with the prerequisites for achieving high capacity and sustained cycling in electrochemical devices. In Fig. 5 f, the EIS curves corresponding to laser treatments conducted at three different durations (3, 6, & 9 min) distinctly illustrate the time-dependent nature of the treatment's impact, wherein prolonged exposure leads to a reduction in the desired ideal Electrical Double Layer (EDL) behavior. Prolonged laser irradiation on the electrode surface proves ineffective in generating additional oxygen defect sites, as evidenced by the diminished effect, notably observed within the semi-circle's mid-frequency region. This phenomenon mirrors the polarization effect observed in the EIS curve of the untreated bare ZCO. Among the three curves, the EIS characteristics of the ZCO@f-MWCNT laser treated for 6 min exhibit the most favorable electrode resistive traits. Specifically, the corresponding Rs values are 1.06 Ω, 0.6 Ω, and 0.68 Ω, while the Rct values are 1.48 Ω, 0.65 Ω, and 1.66 Ω for the respective laser exposure durations of 3, 6, and 9 min. Notably, the complex impedance factor Z" experiences a significant reduction with the 6-minute laser exposure, signifying more suitable and acceptable EDL behavior compared to the other two exposure times. Here, it is concluded that both the excessively brief and overly extended laser exposures are found to be favorable for generating oxygen defects.

High-Performance Hybrid Flexible Supercapacitor (HFSC) device was fabricated by using the synthesized Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT electrode material (2.4 mg) as Cathode and Activated Carbon (7.1 mg) as Anode and integrated with a PVA-KOH flexible solid-state gel electrolyte. The HFSC was fabricated to operate between 0.0 and 1.6 V designed by using individual electrode operating windows of CV and GCD analysis in Fig. 6 . As the full cell performance is the combined performance of anode and cathode, the purchased activated carbon was analyzed its electrochemical characterizations through CV, GCD between −1.0 and 0.0 V with various scan rate 10–100 mV s−1 and 1–5 A g−1 current density, which exhibited a highly rectangular shape CV curve with higher capacitance current of 185 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 current density. The CV curve of HFSC device (Fig. 6 C), remained same like spindle structure even at higher scan rate of 100 mV s−1 [61]. At the initial lower scan rate, the redox behavior with spindle structure is observed upto 40 mV s−1, beyond 50 mV s−1, the slight difference in CV pattern on the anodic scan is discernible. On the other hand, cathodic peaks predominated, indicating efficient and smooth electron transfer across all electrode surfaces, which may be due to the pesudocapacitor nature predominated at high scan rate than the EDLC rectangular shape [62].The CV results of HFSC is expected to obtain battery like performance in the charge/discharge studies. The GCD curves of full cell shown in Fig. 6 d, exhibited EDL-like behavior for the current densities (1 A g−1 to 20 A g−1), at low current density (0.5–1 A g−1) shows slightly redox capacity behavior. All GCD curves exhibited symmetrical form, with Coulombic Efficiency values ranged from 95 % to 100 % for 2 A g−1 to 20 A g−1. At low (0.5 A g−1) and high (20 A g−1) current densities, specific capacity values were 285 F g−1 and 101 F g−1, respectively. The capacity values decreased with increasing charge rate, as electrochemical processes maximized utilization of both diffusion and capacitive electrodes, consistent with the CV results. To assess longevity of the cell, 10,000 cycles at 30 A g−1 current density was studied, the HFSCs device demonstrated a coulombic efficiency (CE) of 95 % and a capacity retention (CR) of 86 % illustrated in Fig. 6 g, confirms its long-term cycle life. The RS and Rct values have not shown any notorious increases even after 10,000 cycles, which were only 1.2 Ω & 9.5 Ω respectively. Despite a negligible differences in Rct value after 10,000 cycles, indicate the electrode material is highly stable, without any structural and morphological changes evident from the SEM pictures presented in Fig. S9, the device maintained excellent electrochemical performance. This observation is consistent with a small deviation from the semi-circle, likely due to ion deterioration. The CR with CE during the cell life test (10,000 cycles) indicated that the cell retained its initial cycle performance. A minor efficiency loss of approximately 5 % occurred due to the redox material's activity, resulting in a limited percentage of ion recovery loss during cycling. This observation aligns with the decrease in capacity retention, attributed to the irreversibility of redox activity, apparent in the mid-frequency region of impedance spectra. Fig. 6 f illustrates the energy density vs. power density at different current density rates calculated by the fabricated HFSC device, achieved a maximum energy density of 101.3 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 400.8 W kg−1. As the current density rate increased, energy density gradually decreased from 85.3 to 35.5 Wh kg−1, while power density progressively increased from 560.6 to 16,000 W kg−1. The plot exhibited typical EDL behavior without sudden value changes. At low current rates, very small power density values were attained due to strong adsorption in the electrode, possibly related to the incomplete recovery or recycling and electrolyte instability. Other than low charge rates, a linear relationship between specific energy and power density was observed. A Ragone plot (Fig. S8) was employed to contextualize the device's energy and power consumption relative to other devices, crucial for comparative evaluation. These values reveal the exceptional energy and power capabilities of HFSCs device, which are higher than previously reported binary spinel metal oxide materials given in the Table 2 [63–69]. The mechanical stability of the fabricated device was assessed by subjecting Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT//AC them to bending tests at various angles (0°, 30°, 60° and 90°), as shown in Fig. 7 a, concurrently with CV tests at scan rate of 10 mV s−1 exhibited the robustness of the fabricated cell with negligible deviation in the CV pattern and capacitive current (Fig. 7 b) [70,71]. Investigation on the feasibility of the High-Performance Hybrid Flexible Supercapacitor (HFSCs) for commercial application was conducted by connecting two and three devices in series (S) and the resulting in operational voltages of 3.2 V (for two devices) and 4.8 V (for three devices) was obtained, illustrated in Fig. 7 c. The GCD curves obtained closely resembled those seen in single-cell configurations, indicating the potential for creating individual devices with consistent performance. Further, the applicability of the fabricated HFSCs have been demonstrated by illuminating a red LED for 7 min indicating the suitability for commercial application. This accomplishment was achieved without notable energy depletion when employing three series connected HFSCs that were charged using a solar simulator. This effective demonstration scores the potential efficacy of these supercapacitors for energy storage applications in the realm of flexible electronics.

In summary, hydrothermally synthesized laser treated ZCO nanoleaves accumulated microspheres of carbon nanodots smeared electrode materials with 201.3 m2 g−1 surface area have been interrogated for supercapacitor applications. Spinel oxygen vacancy created Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6 electrode exhibited higher specific capacitance of 2116 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 current density, other counter parts with and without laser and hydrothermally synthesized at various temperature exhibited lesser specific capacitance. Moreover, an excellent capacity retention of 95 % after 10,000 cycles have been observed for Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6. The fabricated Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6//AC Hybrid Flexible planar Supercapacitor (HFSCs) exhibit energy density 101.3 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 400.8 W kg−1. Meanwhile, the HFSCs device has exhibited outstanding bendability characteristics (at 0°, 30°, 60°, 90°), not even small deviation in the i-V pattern, which confirms its viability as a candidate for high-energy flexible hybrid supercapacitors. Further, practical application evaluation demonstrated that the enhanced HFSCs is exemplified by the successful illumination of a red LED for 7 min. This Ov-ZCO@f-MWCNT-6 electrode material is a promising cathode material for the next-generation flexible energy storage applications by contribute can towards a green economy.

Muthukumar Ganesan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Srinivasan Alagar: Methodology, Data curation. Vivek Bagchi: Formal analysis. Shakkthivel Piraman: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
